title: Graphs: BFS & DFS
description: A Graph is a non-linear data structure consisting of Vertices (nodes) and Edges (connections). Graphs are used to represent networks of communication, data organization, and computational flow.

sections.title: Vertices and Edges
sections.content: Unlike Trees, Graphs can contain cycles (loops) and disconnected components. An edge connecting two vertices can be directed (one-way) or undirected (two-way). Because of cycles, graph traversal algorithms must keep track of 'visited' nodes to avoid infinite loops.

sections.title: Breadth-First Search (BFS)
sections.content: BFS explores the graph level by level, radiating outward from the starting vertex. It visits all immediate neighbors before moving to the next level. BFS uses a Queue (FIFO) data structure and is ideal for finding the shortest path in unweighted networks.

sections.title: Depth-First Search (DFS)
sections.content: DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It plunges deep into the graph using a Stack (LIFO) or recursion. DFS is heavily used for topological sorting, solving mazes, and detecting cycles in a network.
